source_book: NCERT Class 11 Physics Part I + HC Verma Ch.5

real_world_anchor.primary: You stand on the classroom floor — the floor pushes straight up on your feet. Press your palm against a wall — the wall pushes horizontally on your hand. When the surface changes direction, the normal force changes direction with it.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A ladder leans against a wall in your building. The ground pushes the ladder up. The wall pushes the ladder sideways. Two contact surfaces give two different normal directions.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A book rests on a tilted desk. The normal force from the desk is perpendicular to the desk surface — not straight up. N = mg cos(theta), always perpendicular to that specific surface.

constraints: N is ALWAYS perpendicular to the contact surface
N is ALWAYS directed away from the surface (outward)
N can never be negative — surfaces push, they do not pull
On an incline of angle theta from horizontal, N = mg cos(theta)
On a flat horizontal surface (theta = 0), N reduces to mg
In an accelerating frame, use N - mg = ma → N = m(g + a)
When the contact is lost (free fall, apex of projectile from a surface), N = 0

STATE_1.title: You Stand on the Floor — The Floor Pushes Back. In Which Direction?

scene_composition.text:
Gravity pulls you
down at 588 N

Floor pushes back —
but in which direction?

tts_sentences.text_en: You are standing on the classroom floor. Gravity pulls you down with 588 Newtons.
You are not falling, which means the floor must be pushing back on you with an equal force.
This push from the floor has a direction. What direction is it in?
This force is called Normal Reaction. And the word Normal has a very specific mathematical meaning.

scene_composition.text:
'Normal' in mathematics
means PERPENDICULAR.
N points at 90 degrees
to the contact surface.

A surface can only PUSH,
never pull. So N always
points AWAY from the surface.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the key insight. Normal does not mean ordinary or usual.
In mathematics, Normal means perpendicular. Normal Reaction is the force perpendicular to the contact surface.
It always points away from the surface, because a surface can push, but it cannot pull.
Perpendicular to the surface, directed outward. That is the complete definition of N. Tap continue when you are ready.

STATE_3.title: Tilt the Surface — Which Way Does N Point Now?

scene_composition.text:
N tilts WITH the surface —
still perpendicular to it.
N = mg cos30 = 17.0 N

mg stays vertical.
Gravity never tilts with
the surface.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now tilt the surface to 30 degrees. Before I reveal the answer — think carefully.
The block is sitting on a tilted surface. In which direction does the normal reaction N point now?
Pick one of the four options and I will tell you why.

retrieval_question.prompt: The surface is tilted to 30 degrees from horizontal. In which direction does N point?

options.label: Straight up (vertical)
options.feedback: That is mg's direction, not N's. N is perpendicular to the surface, not to gravity. Once the surface tilts, N tilts with it.

options.label: Perpendicular to the incline (tilted 30 degrees from vertical)
options.feedback: Correct. N is always perpendicular to its contact surface. When the surface tilts by 30 degrees, N tilts by 30 degrees too.

options.label: Along the incline (parallel to the slope)
options.feedback: That is mg sin(theta) — the component of gravity along the slope. N is the perpendicular component direction, not the parallel one.

options.label: It depends on whether the block is sliding
options.feedback: N's direction depends only on the surface orientation, not on the block's motion. Motion changes friction, not N.

STATE_4.title: Ladder Against a Wall — Two Surfaces, Two Normals

scene_composition.text:
N1 from ground —
vertical (perpendicular
to the floor)

N2 from wall —
horizontal (perpendicular
to the vertical wall)

tts_sentences.text_en: A ladder leans against the wall of your building. There are two contact surfaces.
From the ground, N one is vertical upward — perpendicular to the horizontal floor.
From the wall, N two is horizontal, pointing away from the wall — perpendicular to the vertical wall.
Two surfaces, two different normal directions. The rule never changes — N is always perpendicular to its own surface.

STATE_5.title: Your Turn — Drag the Sliders to See N = mg cos(theta)

tts_sentences.text_en: Now it is your turn. Drag the two sliders — change mass and angle together.
Set theta to zero. Watch what happens. N equals mg. Flat surface, both forces equal and opposite.
Now increase theta. Watch N shrink. More weight goes along the slope, less perpendicular to it.
Take theta to 90 degrees. N becomes zero. The surface is vertical — it gives no perpendicular support. The formula always works.

epic_c_branches.misconception: N is always equal to mg regardless of the surface
trigger_phrases: normal force is always equal to weight
N and mg are the same thing
why is N less than mg on an incline

scene_composition.text:
If N = mg always,
then N must be 19.6 N
even on the incline.
But is that really true?

tts_sentences.text_en: Let us first see your belief clearly. You think N equals mg always — flat surface, incline, any surface.
So on a 30 degree incline, you say N should still equal 19.6 Newtons — the full weight.
That feels intuitive. But there is a problem with this — let me show you what goes wrong.

EPIC_C_2.title: Why It Fails — Decompose mg into Components

scene_composition.text:
Only the PERPENDICULAR
component of mg pushes
into the surface.
N balances only that part.

The PARALLEL part
(mg sin30 = 9.8 N)
slides the block down —
it is not balanced by N.

tts_sentences.text_en: Split mg into two parts. One perpendicular to the incline, one parallel to it.
Perpendicular component is mg cos30, which equals 17 Newtons. Parallel component is mg sin30, which equals 9.8 Newtons.
Only the perpendicular component pushes into the surface. The parallel part tries to slide the block down.
N balances only the perpendicular part. So N equals mg cos30, not mg. That is 17 Newtons, not 19.6.

scene_composition.text:
RULE:
N = mg cos(theta)
(theta = angle of incline)

Flat surface: theta = 0
cos(0) = 1
N = mg ✓ (special case)

30 deg incline:
cos(30) = 0.866
N = 0.866 * mg
N = 17.0 N (not 19.6)

Vertical wall: theta = 90
cos(90) = 0
N = 0 (no support)

tts_sentences.text_en: The rule is N equals mg cos theta. This rule always works.
On a flat surface, theta is zero, cosine of zero is one, so N equals mg. Your old belief was this special case.
On a 30 degree incline, N is 17 Newtons — less than the weight.
On a vertical wall, N is zero — the surface gives no support. One rule, every case.

epic_c_branches.misconception: N and mg are a Newton's third law action-reaction pair
trigger_phrases: N and mg are action reaction pair
newton third law gives N equals mg
are N and mg the pair

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: N and mg Are Newton's Third Law Partners

scene_composition.text:
Your belief:
N and mg are equal,
opposite, and both act on
the block. So they must be
Newton's third law pair.
RIGHT?

tts_sentences.text_en: You see two forces on the block — mg pulls it down, N pushes it up.
They are equal in magnitude. They are opposite in direction.
So it feels natural to call them Newton's third law pair — action and reaction.
But this is the most common mistake in physics. Let me show you why they are not a third law pair.

EPIC_C_2.title: The Test: Third Law Pairs Act on DIFFERENT Bodies

scene_composition.text:
Third law rule:
Pairs act on DIFFERENT
bodies.

N acts on block ←→
N' acts on floor.

mg acts on block ←→
mg' acts on Earth.

tts_sentences.text_en: Newton's third law pairs always act on two different bodies.
N acts on the block, pushing up. Its real partner is the block pushing down on the floor. That is a pair.
mg acts on the block, pulling down. Its real partner is the block pulling Earth up. That is a different pair.
N and mg both act on the same body — the block. They cannot be a third law pair. They are just two separate forces that happen to balance.

EPIC_C_3.title: Two Separate Forces on Same Body — Second Law Balance

scene_composition.text:
FACT 1:
N and mg act on
the SAME body (block).
They balance by
Newton's SECOND law:
Sum of forces = 0

FACT 2:
N's real third law pair
is the block pushing
the floor downward.
That force acts on
the FLOOR.

SHORTCUT:
If two forces act on
the SAME body, they are
NEVER a third law pair.
They can only balance
by the second law.

tts_sentences.text_en: Two facts to remember. N and mg act on the same body — the block.
Same body means they are not a third law pair. They balance each other by Newton's second law.
N's real third law partner is the block pushing down on the floor — that force acts on the floor, not on the block.
Shortcut rule: if two forces act on the same body, they are never a third law pair.

epic_c_branches.misconception: N changes when the block slides horizontally on the floor
trigger_phrases: does N change when block moves

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: N Decreases When the Block Slides

scene_composition.text:
Your belief:
Block is moving fast,
so N must shrink or change
because the block is
'pushing less' into the floor.

tts_sentences.text_en: You think — the block is sliding at 3 meters per second. Maybe N is smaller because of the motion.
Or maybe N changes in some way because of the velocity.
Let me show you why N has nothing to do with horizontal motion.

EPIC_C_2.title: The Truth: N Balances Only the Vertical Direction

scene_composition.text:
VERTICAL axis:
Forces here: N (up) and mg (down).
Block does not move vertically.
So N = mg.

HORIZONTAL axis:
Motion happens here.
Forces here: only friction
(or none, if smooth).
N has NO role.

tts_sentences.text_en: Split the problem into two directions. Vertical and horizontal.
Vertical direction — forces are N up and mg down. The block is not moving vertically. So N equals mg.
Horizontal direction — motion happens here. Forces are friction, or nothing if smooth. N does not act here at all.
Motion is horizontal. N is vertical. They are in different directions. Horizontal motion cannot change a vertical force.

EPIC_C_3.title: When Does N Actually Change? Only Vertical Acceleration

scene_composition.text:
N changes only when
the block ACCELERATES
VERTICALLY.

Block slides horizontally
(smooth or rough):
vertical a = 0
N = mg (unchanged)

Elevator up at 2 m/s^2:
vertical a = 2 m/s^2
N = m(g + a)
N is LARGER

Free fall:
vertical a = g downward
N = 0 (no contact
force at all)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the rule. N changes only when the block accelerates vertically.
Horizontal motion, even at very high speed, does not change N. No vertical acceleration means no change.
In an elevator accelerating upward, N increases. In free fall, N becomes zero.
Simple test — ask yourself: is there vertical acceleration? If no, N equals mg no matter what happens horizontally.

epic_c_branches.misconception: In an elevator, N is always equal to mg regardless of elevator motion
trigger_phrases: why do I feel heavier in elevator

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: N = mg Even in a Moving Elevator

scene_composition.text:
Your belief:
Elevator goes up at
2 m/s^2, but you still
weigh 60 kg, so
N must equal mg = 588 N.

tts_sentences.text_en: An elevator accelerates upward at 2 meters per second squared. You are standing inside.
You think — my mass is still 60 kilograms, gravity is still 9.8. So N must still be 588 Newtons.
But you know from experience that you feel heavier when a lift starts going up. Why?
Let me show you what N actually is in an accelerating frame.

EPIC_C_2.title: Newton's Second Law in the Elevator: N - mg = ma

scene_composition.text:
Newton's second law:
N - mg = ma

Solve for N:
N = m(g + a)
N = 60 × (9.8 + 2)
N = 60 × 11.8
N = 708 N

tts_sentences.text_en: Apply Newton's second law. Net force equals mass times acceleration.
Upward forces minus downward forces equals m times a. So N minus mg equals m a.
Solve for N. You get N equals m times g plus a. That is 60 times 11.8, which is 708 Newtons.
N is larger than mg by exactly m times a. This is why you feel heavier when the lift starts moving up.

EPIC_C_3.title: Every Elevator Case — Free Fall Included

scene_composition.text:
RULE: N = m(g + a)
(a = + up, a = − down)

Elevator UP
at 2 m/s^2:
a = +2
N = m(9.8 + 2)
N = 708 N
(heavier)

Elevator DOWN
at 2 m/s^2:
a = −2
N = m(9.8 − 2)
N = 468 N
(lighter)

Rest / constant v:
a = 0
N = mg
N = 588 N
(normal weight)

Cable snaps
(free fall):
a = −9.8
N = m(9.8 − 9.8)
N = 0
(weightless)

tts_sentences.text_en: Remember one formula — N equals m times g plus a. Sign of a depends on the direction.
Elevator accelerating upward: a is positive. N is larger than mg. You feel heavier.
Elevator accelerating downward: a is negative. N is smaller than mg. You feel lighter.
If the cable snaps — free fall — a equals minus g. N becomes zero. You feel weightless. This is also why astronauts float in orbit.

regeneration_variants.label: Cylinder in V-groove
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Heavy cylinder resting inside a V-shaped groove — two inclined surfaces, N1 and N2 both perpendicular to their respective surfaces
regeneration_variants.same_physics: N is always perpendicular to its contact surface, directed outward. Multiple contacts mean multiple N forces.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Two inclined walls forming a V-shape, a cylinder inside, N1 from the left wall, N2 from the right wall — each perpendicular to its own surface

regeneration_variants.label: Three Stacked Blocks
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Three blocks stacked vertically on the floor — N1 between block A and block B, N2 between block B and block C, N3 between block C and the floor
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Each interface has its own N perpendicular to that specific contact surface. The bottom N supports the full combined weight.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Three cuboids stacked, N arrows drawn at each interface, each labeled with the weight it supports from above

regeneration_variants.label: Book on Tilted Desk
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Textbook resting on a 15 degree tilted desk in your classroom
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same N = mg cos(theta) formula as the main concept, with different numerical values
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Desk tilted at 15 deg, textbook on top, N perpendicular to desk surface

STATE_1.description: Identify the contact surface and draw the free body diagram with mg and N.

STATE_2.description: State the definition of N — perpendicular to the contact surface, directed outward.

STATE_3.description: Decompose mg into parallel and perpendicular components on an incline.

STATE_4.description: Identify multiple contact surfaces and draw all N forces — one per surface.

STATE_5.description: State and verify the formula N = mg cos(theta) with correct numerical substitution.

primitives.text: Step 1: Identify the body — a 60 kg person on the classroom floor.
Step 2: Contact surface — horizontal floor.
Step 3: Free body diagram — mg downward, N upward.
+1 for FBD
Definition: N is the force perpendicular to the contact surface,
directed away from the surface (since surfaces push, not pull).
On a flat horizontal surface: N is vertical, N = mg.
+1 for definition
On an incline of angle theta, resolve mg into two components:
Perpendicular to incline: mg cos(theta)
Parallel to incline: mg sin(theta)
+1 for decomposition
Multiple contacts → one N per surface.
Ladder example: N1 from ground (vertical), N2 from wall (horizontal).
Each N is perpendicular to its own surface.
+1 for multi-surface
Formula: N = mg cos(theta)
Substitute: m = 2 kg, theta = 30 deg, g = 9.8 m/s^2
+1 for formula use

shortcuts.use_when: No incline, no elevator acceleration, no applied vertical force

shortcuts.rule: Incline of angle theta from horizontal
shortcuts.use_when: Block rests on or slides along an inclined surface without vertical acceleration
a is positive if accelerating upward, negative if accelerating downward; a = 0 at rest or constant velocity
Object and supporting surface both in free fall — no contact force

shortcuts.formula: N = mg + F (F pressing down) or N = mg - F (F pulling up, F < mg)
shortcuts.use_when: An extra vertical force is applied to the object besides its weight

edge_cases.title: Block on wedge, wedge on frictionless table
edge_cases.trap: Students forget the wedge can slide, which changes the block's acceleration relative to ground.
edge_cases.resolution: Write N equations in the ground frame, not the wedge frame. The block's acceleration has both horizontal and vertical components.

edge_cases.title: Ball inside a vertical circular loop
edge_cases.trap: Students apply N = mg at the top of the loop.
edge_cases.resolution: At the top, N and mg both point toward center (downward). N + mg = mv^2/r. Minimum speed occurs when N = 0, giving v_min = sqrt(gr).

edge_cases.title: Banked road turn (no friction)
edge_cases.resolution: N has both vertical and horizontal components. Vertical: N cos(theta) = mg. Horizontal: N sin(theta) = mv^2/r. Combine: tan(theta) = v^2/(rg).

edge_cases.title: Person in a freely falling elevator holding a ball
edge_cases.trap: Students think the person still pushes the ball upward with their hand.
edge_cases.resolution: Everything accelerates at g — the person, the ball, the elevator. N between person's hand and ball is zero. Ball appears to float.

edge_cases.title: Your apparent weight at the equator
edge_cases.trap: Ignoring Earth's rotation when asked for N on a weighing scale.
edge_cases.resolution: N_measured = mg - m*omega^2*R at equator. Small correction (~0.3%), but classic JEE trap.
